cards.text: Unary operators take {{c1::zero}} parameters as member functions (operate on *this) or {{c2::one}} parameter as friend/normal functions.

cards.front: What are the three common unary operators to overload?
cards.back:
• Unary minus (operator-): negation
  • Unary plus (operator+): rarely needed
  • Logical NOT (operator!): boolean inversion

cards.question: What does this unary operator- do?
cards.answer: Returns a new Cents object with the negated value. Allows -myObj syntax for negation.
cards.explanation: Unary minus creates a new object with negated value. Marked const since it doesn't modify the original object.

cards.text: Unary operator- should be marked {{c1::const}} and return a {{c2::new object}} by value, not modify *this.

cards.front: When would you overload operator! for a class?
cards.back: When you want to provide a boolean test for the class. For example, operator! could return true if a container is empty or if a pointer is null. Should return a bool value.

cards.text: operator! should return {{c1::bool}} and is typically used to test if an object is in a {{c2::false-like}} state (empty, null, invalid, etc.).

cards.question: How would you use this operator! overload?
cards.answer: if (!myVector) { /* vector is empty */ } or while (!vec) { }
cards.explanation: operator! returns true when the vector is empty, allowing intuitive boolean tests.

cards.front: Should unary operator- modify the object it's called on?
cards.back: No. Unary operator- should return a new object with the negated value, leaving the original unchanged. It should be const. If you want to negate in-place, use a different function like negate().

cards.concept: Best practices for unary operator overloading
cards.explanation:
Guidelines for unary operators:
  • operator-: Return new negated object, mark const
  • operator+: Rarely needed, returns copy
  • operator!: Return bool, test for invalid/empty/false state
  • Don't modify the object (use const)
  • Return appropriate types (object for -, bool for !)

cards.text: As a member function, unary operator- has signature: ClassName {{c1::operator-}}() {{c2::const}} with {{c3::no parameters}}.
